title: Methodology
text:
Architecture
The site has three coupled layers:

Static Quarto site — all content pages (course catalogue, designation pathways, learning outcomes, skills scaffold, methodology, announcements). Authored in markdown / qmd; rendered to HTML and served as static files.
Shiny degree planner — the interactive component, embedded as an iframe in the static site. Lets a student drag courses into year slots, pick a designation goal, and see real-time gap analysis.
targets data pipeline — daily scrape of the UBCO Academic Calendar, normalisation to a tidy parquet, plus the curated designation-mapping YAMLs. Output artefacts feed both the Quarto site (via embedded chunks) and the Shiny planner.



Data sources and refresh cadence









Feed
Source URL
How
Cadence




Course descriptions
okanagan.calendar.ubc.ca/course-descriptions/subject/<code>
rvest scrape, per-subject
Daily, 12:00 UTC


Program structure
okanagan.calendar.ubc.ca/.../freshwater-science
rvest scrape
Daily, 12:00 UTC


Course schedule (offered next year?)
UBCO Workday Student / SSC
TBD — scrape vs. API
Daily, 12:00 UTC


BCIA PAg UBCO-approved list
bcia.com/sites/.../UBCO Approved Courses_*.pdf
Manual OCR + curated YAML
Triggered on PDF update (BCIA edits the file ~annually)


CAB RPBio Credentialing Standard
cab-bc.org/wp-content/uploads/Credentialing_Standard_*.pdf
Manual OCR + curated YAML
Triggered on standard update


Program learning outcomes
Faculty of Science / EESC departmental docs
Manual transcription
Annual


Course learning outcomes
Instructor interviews
Manual collection
Per-course, on rolling basis





Scrape policy
UBCO’s calendar is publicly accessible. The scraper respects the following constraints:

Single sequential scrape (no parallel requests) with a polite User-Agent: limnology.ca UBCO Freshwater Pathways v0.x.
Per-request delay of 1 second between subject pages.
Local cache (file mtime check) so a re-run within the same day is a no-op.
All scraping happens server-side in GitHub Actions, never in the user’s browser.

If UBCO’s registrar office can provide a data feed or API, the scraper will be retired in favour of that.


Designation-mapping authority
The PAg and RPBio mappings are not scraped. The source documents (BCIA UBCO list, CAB Credentialing Standard) are PDF publications by the respective regulatory bodies. The workflow:

Download the latest PDF when published.
OCR locally to a text file in docs/reference/.
Hand-curate the UBCO course-code -> designation-category map into data/designation_mappings/pag.yml and .../rpbio.yml.
Versioned, dated YAMLs so historical pathways are preserved.

This preserves traceability: every course-counts-for-X claim on the site is backed by a citable, dated source.


Pipeline reproducibility
The targets pipeline plus the GitHub Actions cron workflow give:

Full provenance for every artefact (which scrape run, which source version, which curator).
Deterministic re-renders.
Public commit history of when designation mappings changed.



Stack inventory







Component
Library




Site
Quarto 1.6+


Scraping
rvest, httr2, xml2


Tidying
dplyr, tidyr, stringr, lubridate


Storage
arrow (parquet)


Pipeline
targets


Planner
shiny, bslib, DT, visNetwork (prereq graph)


Validation
assertr, testthat


Deploy
GitHub Actions, shinyapps.io (planner), limnology.ca (static site)

title: Skills scaffolding
text:
WarningScaffold placeholder



Skills are stored in skills/skills_vocab.yml (the controlled vocabulary) and skills/course_tags.yml (per-course tagging with introduce/practise/expect markers). Neither file exists yet. The heatmap viz on this page is the eventual rendering of those YAMLs.



The idea
Skills get scaffolded through a degree: introduced in one course, practised in several, and expected as background in upper-year courses. Without an explicit map, neither students nor instructors can see this arc clearly.
This page shows the scaffolded coverage as a heatmap: rows are skills from a controlled vocabulary, columns are the four years of the program, cells are coloured by introduce / practise / expect status.


Controlled skill vocabulary (draft)
Three top-level skill families to start, each with sub-skills. These are stubs; the real vocabulary will be co-developed with department instructors.


Quantitative / computational

R basics
R intermediate (tidyverse)
R for hydrology (tidyhydat, dataRetrieval)
Statistical inference
Geostatistics
Spatial analysis (sf, terra)
Modelling (GLM, GAM)



Field / lab

Sampling design
Water-quality sampling
Macroinvertebrate identification
Hydrometric measurement
Lab safety
Calibration and QC



Communication / professional

Scientific writing
Public-facing communication
Peer review
Project management
Ethics
Working with First Nations





Pending content

Co-develop the controlled skill vocabulary with the program instructor cohort.
Tag each Freshwater Science course with the skills it introduces / practises / expects.
Render the skills-by-year heatmap.

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
RPBio = Registered Professional Biologist, granted by the College of Applied Biologists — the regulatory body for applied biology professionals in BC. Entry-level is Biologist in Training (BIT) with the academic credential complete; RPBio follows after validated experience, competencies, and submitted work products.


A bachelor’s degree (or master’s) with:

25 science courses, 13 in biology.
Required core courses across:

Communications (typically first-year writing)
Chemistry
Numeracy (math)
Statistics at 2nd-year or higher
Applied biology / management
Ecology
Three of five from: genetics, cell biology, physiology, systematics/taxonomy, evolution

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
A bachelor’s degree (or master’s) with:

25 science courses, 13 in biology.
Required core courses across:

Communications (typically first-year writing)
Chemistry
Numeracy (math)
Statistics at 2nd-year or higher
Applied biology / management
Ecology
Three of five from: genetics, cell biology, physiology, systematics/taxonomy, evolution

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
What’s already covered

Communications: first-year writing block (ENGL or program-approved alternatives).
Chemistry: CHEM 121/123, 201/210/211, 301 — comfortable coverage.
Numeracy: MATH 100/101.
Statistics (≥2nd-year): STAT 230 or GEOG 271 or BIOL 202.
Ecology / applied biology: BIOL 201 (Ecology & Evolution), BIOL 308 (Population Biology), FWSC/BIOL 375 (Flora & Fauna of Inland Waters).

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Electives to close gaps
To reach the 13-biology count and the three-of-five subject areas:

Cell biology → BIOL 200
Genetics → BIOL 265
Physiology → BIOL 356 or BIOL 358
Systematics / taxonomy → BIOL 371
Evolution → BIOL 201 (already in the core)

Pick whichever combination of three covers your gap without doubling up on areas you already hold.

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
section: Sample 4-year plan for the RPBio track
text:
Sample 4-year plan for the RPBio track
Illustrative; verify with the program advisor and current CAB rules.






NoteYear 1






T1: BIOL 116; CHEM 121 or 123; MATH 100; WRDS/ENGL/COMM; elective.
T2: BIOL 125; the other of CHEM 121/123; PHYS 111 or 112; elective.










NoteYear 2






T1: BIOL 201; STAT 230 / GEOG 271 / BIOL 202 (≥2nd-year stats); EESC 205 (Hydrology); elective.
T2: EESC 222 (Geomorphology); CHEM 211; BIOL 200 (Cell); elective.










NoteYear 3






T1: EESC 301 (Limnology); FWSC/BIOL 375 (Flora & Fauna of Inland Waters); CHEM 301; elective.
T2: BIOL 265 (Genetics); BIOL 356 or 358 (Physiology) or BIOL 371 (Systematics); BIO upper-level.










NoteYear 4






T1: EESC 402 (Freshwater Resources); pick 1-2 of EESC 309 / 323 / 423 (geochem requirement); BIO upper-level (reaching the 13-BIO total).
T2: EESC 342 (Hydrogeology) or EESC 413 (Analytical Methods); BIO upper-level; Directed Studies / Field / Special Topics.

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Co-op and experience
Co-op terms after Year 2 (summer start) for 8-12 months, or after Year 3 summer. Target biomonitoring, fisheries, environmental consulting, and government. Experience accumulated during BIT counts toward the RPBio bridge.

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Seat / prerequisite cautions

Some 3xx BIOL seats prioritise Biology majors. Request permission early.
BIOL 200, 265, 356, 358, 371 are the gap-filler electives — plan registration so they aren’t squeezed by required EESC courses.

title: RPBio — Registered Professional Biologist (College of Applied Biologists)
text:
Professional work products
To convert BIT → RPBio you must submit at least one scientific or risk-assessment report that meets College criteria, plus at least one public-facing communication piece (annotated presentation, briefing note, interpretation document, etc.). Directed Studies, Honours Thesis, and co-op term reports are the natural vehicles for these.

title: Complementary pathways
text:
Many students who pick Freshwater Science want a focus on top of the broad core — an applied direction they can take into work or graduate school. The patterns below come from the program lead’s curated advising map (docs/reference/advising/Map UBCO Freshwater Science (BSc) - Pathways.docx). Use them as starting points; the program advisor will sharpen the specific course choices to your goals.

Water quality and biogeochemistry
A Chemistry-leaning track that builds applied analytical and contaminant-cycling expertise.

Core EESC: EESC 301 (Limnology), EESC 309 (Global Biogeochemistry), EESC 423 (Tracers of Natural Processes).
Core CHEM: CHEM 211 (Analytical), CHEM 301 (Aqueous Environmental Chemistry).
GISC Minor to add spatial analysis and remote sensing.
Suits: contaminant monitoring, redox / speciation work, trace analysis in waters, water-utility lab roles.
Lab home: UBCO Chemistry FiLTER (Field Lab for Trace Element Research) is a frequent collaborator.



Aquatic ecology and conservation
A Biology-leaning track for biomonitoring, fisheries, and species-level work.

Core EESC: EESC 301 (Limnology), EESC 402 (Freshwater Resources).
Core BIOL: BIOL 308 (Population Biology), BIOL 375 / FWSC 375 (Flora & Fauna of Inland Waters).
Year-4 capstone: Directed Studies or EESC 449 (Honours Thesis).
Co-op targets: provincial and federal biology agencies, fisheries consulting, NGOs.
Designation fit: this is the natural pairing with the RPBio pathway.



Hydrology and water resources
A Civil-Engineering-adjacent track for watershed modelling, flood/drought risk, and water infrastructure.

Core EESC: EESC 413 (Analytical Methods in Hydrology), EESC 435 (Fluvial Field Techniques), EESC 423 (Tracers), EESC 342 (Hydrogeology).
ENGR pairing: ENGR 342 (Open Channel Flow) — seat and prerequisite limits apply; coordinate with Engineering advising.
GISC Minor for geospatial modelling.
Co-op targets: hydrometric agencies (ECCC, WSC), engineering consulting (NHC, Associated Engineering, etc.), utilities.



Practical notes for mixing and matching

Co-op across programs. Co-op is available in Freshwater, Chemistry, Biology, and Engineering — ideal for stitching together applied experience.
Credit double-counting. The UBCO Academic Calendar caps double-counting of upper-level credits toward a second major or minor (generally max 6 credits for program-specified requirements). Schedule intentionally if you’re aiming for a double credential.
Advising. Use the official Program Plan and meet program advisors early — especially if you’re eyeing Honours, co-op, or cross-faculty electives.



References

UBCO Freshwater Science (program page)
UBC Academic Calendar — Freshwater Science requirements
UBCO BSc Freshwater Science — Program Plan (PDF)
UBCO Chemistry — undergraduate page
UBCO Civil Engineering — programs
UBCO GISC Minor
Source notes in this repo: docs/reference/advising/Map UBCO Freshwater Science (BSc) - Pathways.docx.

title: UBCO Freshwater Science
text:
Welcome to the planning hub for the Freshwater Science undergraduate program at the UBC Okanagan campus. This site helps you:

Browse the courses that count toward Freshwater Science, with current-year prerequisites, equivalencies, and offering status.
Plan a path to the PAg (BC Institute of Agrologists) or RPBio (College of Applied Biology) professional designation alongside your degree.
Track learning outcomes at the program, department, and individual course level.
See where each skill is scaffolded across the four-year arc (from “intro to R” to “designing a field study”).
Use the interactive planner to test course combinations against your degree requirements and designation goals.







The interactive Planner is in development; preview the design intent on its placeholder page.
Check the PAg pathway and RPBio pathway to see what gaps you still need to fill before graduation.
Browse the course catalogue for prerequisites and “is this offered next year?” signals.
The department’s official page lives at eesc.ok.ubc.ca → Freshwater Science.





Read the official program description at UBC Undergraduate Admissions → Freshwater Science.
Skim the program overview below.
Compare the designation pathways to see what professional qualifications this degree can lead to.
Browse the first-year courses to get a feel for the workload.






The Freshwater Science program integrates aquatic biology, chemistry, geography, and earth and environmental sciences. Students study water quality and quantity, aquatic organisms, and the health of aquatic ecosystems.
The four-year B.Sc. requires 120 credits with at least 78 in Science, at least 42 at the upper level (300+), and at least 36 of those upper-level credits in Science. The Honours track adds a research thesis (EESC_O 449).
Official UBCO pages:

UBC Undergraduate Admissions — Freshwater Science (program overview for prospective students)
Department of Earth, Environmental and Geographic Sciences — Freshwater Science (department page; current-student facing)
UBC Academic Calendar — Freshwater Science requirements (authoritative degree requirements)







NoteSource of truth



Course descriptions and prerequisites are pulled daily from the UBC Okanagan Academic Calendar. Designation requirements are curated from the BCIA UBCO Approved Course List and the CAB Credentialing Standard by the program lead. The Methodology page details the data pipeline and refresh cadence.








Freshwater Science Program Advisor, UBC Okanagan
Email for advising appointments, course planning, designation questions, Honours / Co-op / NSERC USRA enquiries, or anything else.
📧 nelson.jatel@ubc.ca





Built and maintained by Limnology Research Corp on behalf of the program lead, Nelson R. Jatel, PAg. Source code at github.com/DrNelsonJatel/UBCO_Freshwater_Science_Program. Issues and suggestions welcome.

title: PAg — Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
PAg = Professional Agrologist, the senior tier of the BC Institute of Agrologists. You first qualify as an Articling Agrologist (AAg) straight from your degree, then complete BCIA’s Articling Program plus two years of agrology-related work experience to bridge to PAg.


A four-year accredited B.Sc. with a science focus, where the coursework includes:

At least 8 entry-level foundational courses (100/200-level) in the subjects on BCIA’s Academic Worksheet.
At least 20 courses in agriculture, natural sciences, or agriculture/resource economics that relate to agrology under the Agrologists Regulation, 2021.
At least 8 senior-level courses (drawn from the 20 above) in those same subject areas.

Eligible course categories:

Agrology
Foundational Natural Science
Mathematics or Statistics
Economics
Communications / Writing
Computer Science

title: PAg — Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
A four-year accredited B.Sc. with a science focus, where the coursework includes:

At least 8 entry-level foundational courses (100/200-level) in the subjects on BCIA’s Academic Worksheet.
At least 20 courses in agriculture, natural sciences, or agriculture/resource economics that relate to agrology under the Agrologists Regulation, 2021.
At least 8 senior-level courses (drawn from the 20 above) in those same subject areas.

Eligible course categories:

Agrology
Foundational Natural Science
Mathematics or Statistics
Economics
Communications / Writing
Computer Science

title: PAg — Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
What’s already covered

Foundations (8 required): Year 1 science (BIOL 116, CHEM 113 or 121/123, MATH 100, PHYS 111/112 or 112/122) plus EESC 205 (Hydrology) and EESC 222 (Geomorphology) cover the foundational natural-science count.
Communications / Writing: the program’s 6-credit English / communications block satisfies BCIA’s communications requirement.
Mathematics / Statistics: MATH 100 + STAT 230 / GEOG 271 / BIOL 202.

title: PAg — Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
section: Suggested electives to satisfy the 20-course / 8-senior requirement
text:
Suggested electives to satisfy the 20-course / 8-senior requirement
From BCIA’s UBCO-specific approved list (June 2025), the most relevant electives for a Freshwater student:

EESC: 301, 309, 313, 314, 315, 333, 342, 402, 413, 422, 423, 429, 431, 435
GIS / Geography: GISC 380, GISC 381, GEOG 380, GEOG 381
Economics: ECON 371, 372
Chemistry (advanced): CHEM 301, 302, 317

(See the authoritative BCIA UBCO course list (PDF) for the complete approved list. The pathway documents in this repo at docs/reference/program-pathways/ and docs/reference/advising/ reflect the program lead’s curated picks.)

title: PAg — Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
section: Sample 4-year plan for the PAg track
text:
Sample 4-year plan for the PAg track
Illustrative; verify with the program advisor and current BCIA rules.






NoteYear 1






T1: BIOL 116; CHEM 121/123; MATH 100; WRDS/ENGL; elective.
T2: BIOL 125; CHEM 123/121; PHYS 111 or 112; elective.










NoteYear 2






T1: EESC 205 (Hydrology); STAT 230 / GEOG 271 / BIOL 202; ECON 371 or 372 (example); elective.
T2: EESC 222 (Geomorphology); CHEM 211; CHEM 201 or 210 (example); elective.










NoteYear 3






T1: EESC 301 (Limnology); EESC 402 or other policy/management upper-level; GISC/GEOG 380/381 (GIS/Remote Sensing).
T2: Choose among EESC 333, 413, 422, 429, 431, 435; consider CHEM 301/302 as relevant.










NoteYear 4






Round out to ≥20 agrology-related courses with ≥8 at 3rd/4th-year across EESC, BIOL, CHEM, GEOG, GISC, ECON. Add a field or industry project (Directed Studies, Honours Thesis, or co-op term).

title: PAg — Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
Co-op and experience
Co-op terms after Year 2 or Year 3 target utilities, local and regional governments, environmental consulting, and agriculture / watershed NGOs. These count toward the post-degree articling experience required to convert AAg → PAg.

title: PAg — Professional Agrologist (BC Institute of Agrologists)
text:
Seat / prerequisite cautions

GIS courses (GISC 380/381) often have priority rules for majors. Request permission early.
ECON seats fill quickly; plan registration day.
Some upper-level EESC courses alternate years (313/413). Coordinate with the program advisor.

title: Program Learning Outcomes
text:
The current authority for Program Learning Outcomes (PLOs) is the v2 PLO set finalised on 28 April 2026 by Dr. Nelson Jatel, PAg, Lecturer and Program Advisor (memo: docs/reference/learning-outcomes/UBCO_Memo_PLOs_v2_20260428.docx). The v2 set consolidated the v1 draft (14 outcomes) down to 8 outcomes in 4 categories, based on:
The 5-to-7-outcome range is the sector norm; v1’s 14 was flagged by both faculty and benchmarks as too long for meaningful assessment.

title: Program Learning Outcomes
text:
Depth and Breadth of Knowledge

Explain water processes in the context of planetary cycles, surface processes, and environmental systems, including human alterations to freshwater ecosystems.
Describe the physical, chemical, and biological structure and function of freshwater systems.

title: Program Learning Outcomes
text:
Application of Knowledge

Collect, organise, and analyse water and environmental science data using appropriate field, laboratory, computational (e.g., R, Python), and statistical techniques, and represent results in appropriate graphical formats.
Apply the scientific method and quantitative reasoning to develop conceptual models, test hypotheses, and evaluate trade-offs in freshwater management decisions.
Acquire, critically assess, and synthesise freshwater science literature, Indigenous knowledge systems (ways of knowing), data sources, and spatial resources (e.g., maps, remotely sensed data) to inform interpretation and decision-making.
Develop water-related policy options and communicate them effectively to advance uptake by decision-makers.

title: Program Learning Outcomes
text:
Communication

Communicate limnology and environmental concepts to diverse audiences through formal written reports, oral presentations, technical posters, illustrations, and clear field, laboratory, and code documentation.

title: Program Learning Outcomes
text:
Professional Conduct

Demonstrate professional ethics, safe field and laboratory practice, effective teamwork, and respectful engagement with Indigenous Peoples, lands, and rights-holders.

title: Interactive Degree Planner
text:
WarningComing soon



The interactive planner is under active development. The Shiny app at app/app.R in the project source repository is currently a scaffold; it will go live once:

The daily UBCO calendar scraper is implemented (so the planner has live course-offering data).
The PAg and RPBio designation YAMLs are curated from the source PDFs (so the planner can compute live gap analysis against your goals).
A shinyapps.io or Cloud Run deployment is wired up and the _quarto.yml Planner link is repointed at it.

Until then, the static designation pages and the curriculum-map source documents in this repository are the working reference:



What it will do
When live, the planner will let you:

Drag courses into year slots (Year 1, 2, 3, 4 and post-Year-4 summer).
Toggle designation goals (PAg, RPBio, neither, both).
See live gap analysis:

Which prerequisites are missing for the courses you have chosen.
Which BCIA / CAB category requirements are still unfilled.
Whether your total credits clear the 120 / 78 Science / 42 upper-level / 36 upper-level-Science thresholds.

View the prerequisite graph for your selected courses (visNetwork).
Export your plan as a PDF or text summary you can share with the program advisor.



In the meantime

Use the course catalogue for the year-by-year course list and prerequisite notes.
Use the PAg pathway and RPBio pathway for the curated 4-year sample plans + designation-fit narrative.
Use the Complementary pathways page for Chemistry, Biology, and hydrology/water-resources bundles.



Want to help shape it?
If you are a Freshwater Science student or instructor and want input into how the planner should behave — what gap signals matter most, what visual encoding is most useful, how aggressively it should flag prerequisite conflicts — please drop a note to Nelson.Jatel@UBC.ca or open a GitHub issue at the project source repository.
